# suedbycreditcardcompany.com -- Full Content

This file contains the full text content of all pages on suedbycreditcardcompany.com.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## Sued by Credit Card Company? - What to Do [2026]
Source: https://suedbycreditcardcompany.com/

Skip to main content

Sued by Credit Card Company?

Don't Panic. Here's Exactly What to Do.

You Just Got Served - Now What?

Take a breath. Being sued by a credit card company is stressful but it is not an emergency that requires immediate action beyond one thing: note the deadline to respond. You typically have 20-30 days (varies by state) to file an answer with the court. Missing this deadline results in a default judgment.

Step 1: Read the Complaint Carefully

The complaint will state: who is suing you (original creditor or debt buyer), the amount claimed, the account number, and the legal basis. Check every detail. Is the amount correct? Is this actually your account? Has the statute of limitations expired? Check your state's statute of limitations.

Step 2: File an Answer

Even if you owe the money, file an answer. Respond to each paragraph: admit, deny, or state you lack sufficient information. Raise affirmative defenses: statute of limitations, lack of standing (if sued by debt buyer), improper service, incorrect amount.

Many courts have fill-in-the-blank answer forms. Check your local court website.

Step 3: Request Validation

If a debt buyer is suing you (not the original creditor), demand they prove they own the debt. They must produce the original credit agreement, a complete chain of ownership, and an accounting of every charge and payment. Many debt buyers cannot produce these documents. Cease and desist guide.

Common Defenses

Statute of limitations: If the debt is time-barred, the lawsuit fails.

Lack of standing: Debt buyers must prove they own the specific account.

Wrong amount: Challenge interest, fees, and charges that were not in the original agreement.

Identity: Sometimes the wrong person is sued.

Already paid or settled: Bring proof of any payments or settlement agreements.

The Bankruptcy Option

Filing bankruptcy immediately stops the lawsuit through the automatic stay. If you have multiple debts and this lawsuit is just one problem among many, bankruptcy may be the most efficient solution. Chapter 7 eliminates the credit card debt entirely. The automatic stay explained.

How much does bankruptcy cost?

What Happens If You Lose

If a judgment is entered against you, the creditor can garnish wages (up to 25% of disposable income), levy bank accounts, and place liens on property. Bankruptcy can still eliminate judgment debt and stop garnishment even after a judgment is entered. Are you judgment proof?

Frequently Asked Questions

How long do I have to respond to a credit card lawsuit?

Typically 20-30 days from the date you are served, depending on your state. Check the summons for the exact deadline.

Can I negotiate a settlement after being sued?

Yes. Many creditors will settle for 40-60% of the balance even after filing suit. Get any agreement in writing before paying.

What if I cannot afford a lawyer?

Many legal aid organizations help with debt defense. You can also represent yourself (pro se). The court clerk can provide answer forms.

Will bankruptcy stop the lawsuit?

Yes, immediately. The automatic stay halts all litigation the moment you file.

What happens if a credit card company sues me?

The credit card company or a debt buyer files a lawsuit seeking a money judgment. If you do not respond within 20-30 days, the court enters a default judgment allowing wage garnishment up to 25% of disposable income, bank account levies, and property liens.

Can they garnish my wages for credit card debt?

Yes, but only after obtaining a court judgment. Credit card companies cannot garnish wages without suing you first and winning. After a judgment, federal law caps garnishment at 25% of disposable earnings or the amount exceeding 30 times minimum wage, whichever is less.

Should I file bankruptcy if I am being sued?

Bankruptcy may be the best option if you have multiple debts, not just the one lawsuit. Filing immediately stops the suit through the automatic stay. Chapter 7 eliminates credit card debt entirely if you qualify under the means test. Consult a bankruptcy attorney or use a free screener.

Check your bankruptcy discharge eligibility with our free screening tool.

Free Discharge Screener

About This Data: Content based on federal bankruptcy law (Title 11, U.S. Code)
and the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (15 U.S.C. 1692). District-level statistics from the
Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (37.9 million cases, 94 districts, FY 2008-2024).
This is educational content, not legal advice.

## Privacy Policy - suedbycreditcardcompany.com
Source: https://suedbycreditcardcompany.com/privacy.html

suedbycreditcardcompany.com

Home
Privacy

Privacy Policy

How we handle your data. Short version: we don't sell it, share it, or use it for advertising.

Data We Collect

We use Google Analytics (GA4) to measure aggregate site traffic. This includes page views, session duration, approximate geographic region (country and city level), and device type. We do not collect names, email addresses, or personally identifiable information through analytics.

Aggregate event data from Google Analytics is also exported to Google BigQuery for research use. The BigQuery data is used only for internal trajectory analysis and network health reports, is not shared with third parties, and is not used for advertising.

What We Don't Do

We do not sell data. We do not run advertising. We do not collect financial information. We do not use retargeting. We do not share any user data with attorneys, law firms, creditors, debt collectors, or legal service providers.

Specifically, we do not enable Google Signals, Google Ads integration, User-ID collection, or any cross-device tracking feature in Google Analytics. GA4 operates in aggregate-traffic-measurement mode only on this site.

Cookies

We use only first-party cookies set by Google Analytics for aggregate traffic measurement. We do not use advertising cookies, tracking pixels, or third-party analytics cookies.

Third-Party Services

Google Analytics (GA4) - aggregate traffic measurement

Google BigQuery - aggregate event data warehouse for research use only; not shared, not used for advertising

Google Search Console - search query measurement (clicks, impressions, our own page rankings)

GitHub Pages - hosting

Contact Forms & Email

This site may link to contact forms or email addresses operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project. Any data you submit through those channels is handled under the Open Bankruptcy Project master privacy policy, which governs all sites in this network.

Children's Privacy

This site is not directed at children under 13. We do not knowingly collect information from children.

Data Retention

Analytics data is retained for 14 months per Google's standard retention settings. After that window, individual event data is automatically deleted by Google; aggregate report metrics remain available.

Your Rights

You may request deletion of any personal data we hold by contacting info@openbankruptcyproject.org. California residents have additional rights under CCPA. EU residents have additional rights under GDPR. All such requests are handled by the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Network Policy

This site is part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit (determination pending). The same privacy standards apply across every site in the network. The authoritative master policy is maintained at https://openbankruptcyproject.org/privacy.html.

Changes

We may update this policy from time to time. Changes are reflected on the master policy first and then mirrored across all network sites. Check the master policy for the most current version.

Effective: March 30, 2026  |  Updated: April 11, 2026

Home  ·  Privacy  ·  Open Bankruptcy Project

Part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network. Educational information only - not legal advice.

No ads. No affiliate links. No data sold. Supported by donations.
